front: Reading a standard Bluebook case cite left to right (use the neutral example 392 U.S. 1, 30 (1968)), what six components appear and in what order?
back: Case name (parties) - volume number - reporter abbreviation - first page - pinpoint (pin cite) - court & year parenthetical.

front: In the neutral example cite '392 U.S. 1, 30 (1968),' what is the difference between the '1' and the '30'?
back: The '1' is the first page where the opinion starts; the '30' is the pinpoint (pin cite) — the specific page the quoted or relied-on language is on.

front: When must you include a pinpoint (pin cite), and why does a cite without one matter?
back: Always pin when you quote or attribute a specific holding. A cite without a pin is hard to check and weak in a courtroom.

front: Why does a Supreme Court cite's parenthetical carry only the year (e.g., '(1968)') while a lower-court cite names the court (e.g., '(9th Cir. 2021)')?
back: For SCOTUS the reporter 'U.S.' already identifies the court, so the parenthetical is year only; for lower courts the parenthetical must also name the deciding court.

front: Which three reporter abbreviations identify a U.S. Supreme Court opinion?
back: 'U.S.' (official United States Reports), 'S. Ct.' (West), and 'L. Ed. 2d' (Lawyers' Edition) — the official plus its two parallels.

front: Which reporters signal a U.S. Court of Appeals case versus a U.S. District Court case?
back: Courts of Appeals -> 'F.4th'/'F.3d'/'F.2d'/'F.' (with the circuit in the parenthetical); District Courts -> 'F. Supp. 3d'/'F. Supp. 2d'/'F. Supp.'

front: What court and what kind of disposition does the reporter 'F. App'x' (Federal Appendix) signal?
back: Unpublished circuit (Court of Appeals) dispositions — typically non-precedential, persuasive at best.

front: What is the relationship between the reporter series 'F.', 'F.2d', 'F.3d', and 'F.4th'?
back: They are successive editions of the same Federal Reporter that started over as volumes filled up over the decades — 'F.4th' is simply the current one; all are Courts of Appeals reporters.

front: What is a parallel citation, and what should you NOT mistake it for?
back: The same opinion reported in more than one reporter at once (e.g., a SCOTUS case in U.S., S. Ct., and L. Ed. 2d). Don't mistake the three parallels for three different cases.

front: In Bluebook practice, what do the introductory signals '(no signal),' 'see,' 'cf.,' 'e.g.,' and 'but see' each tell the reader?
back: (no signal) = the source directly states the proposition; see = supports by clear inference; cf. = supports an analogous point; e.g. = one example among many that say the same thing; but see = authority that cuts the other way.

front: Short form: '{{c1::Id.}}' refers to the immediately preceding authority, so 'Id. at 30' means {{c2::same source, new page}}.
back: Id. = the immediately preceding authority; 'Id. at 30' = same source, new page. Use only when the cite right before it is the same source.

front: What does 'supra' point to, and why is it generally NOT used for cases under the Bluebook?
back: Supra points back to a source cited earlier but not immediately above; it is used mainly for books, articles, and the like — generally not for cases under Bluebook.

front: What is the practical weight difference between a published and an unpublished federal opinion?
back: Published opinions are precedential — binding on that court and the courts below it within the jurisdiction; unpublished dispositions (often in F. App'x) are typically non-precedential and persuasive at best.

front: Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 32.1 permits citing federal unpublished opinions issued on or after Jan. 1, 2007 — why is that permission not the whole story?
back: Permission to cite is not the same as binding force; an unpublished opinion remains persuasive at best, so confirm a case is published (or otherwise binding in your jurisdiction) before leaning on it.

front: What does 'per curiam' mean, and why can't you judge an opinion's weight from that label alone?
back: Per curiam = 'by the court,' issued in the name of the whole court rather than a single authoring judge. They can be fully precedential (many SCOTUS per curiams are) or summary and non-precedential — judge the weight by the court and whether it's published, not by the 'per curiam' label.

front: Name the free federal case-law research tools an instructor can rely on instead of Westlaw or Lexis.
back: CourtListener (+ RECAP), Google Scholar (case law mode), Justia, Caselaw Access Project (searched via CourtListener), OpenJurist, govinfo, and official court/SCOTUS sites.

front: What is CourtListener's RECAP Archive, and why check it before paying PACER?
back: RECAP is the largest open collection of federal docket filings (briefs, motions, orders) pulled from PACER; the RECAP browser extension captures PACER documents users pull and shares them free, so a filing you need may already be there at no cost.

front: The three ways to search depend on what you already have — what are they?
back: By citation (drop '392 U.S. 1' into a citation lookup for the exact opinion); by party name (search the case name, watching for same-named different cases); by proposition / full text (search the idea, then read down to the controlling case).

front: With no free Shepard's or KeyCite, how do you verify a case is still good law, and what are you scanning for?
back: Do it by hand with free citator substitutes — CourtListener's 'Cited By'/'Authorities' and Google Scholar's 'How cited' — scanning whether later courts followed, criticized, distinguished, or overruled it. A narrowed or reversed case is a trap.

front: Why must you read the actual opinion rather than cite from a headnote or summary?
back: Headnotes are editorial summaries, not the court's words, and can mislead. Pull the text, find the court's actual language, and pin to its page.

front: What is the 'unit of trustworthy legal research' — the triple this wiki saves for every verified case?
back: A consistent Bluebook cite + a CourtListener permalink + a pinpoint (plus a one-line note on the holding and its weight), so the case can be re-found and re-verified instantly.

front: What does the circuit named in a Court of Appeals parenthetical (e.g., '(5th Cir.)') tell you about the case's authority, and what's the pitfall?
back: It tells you whose persuasive authority it is — a Fifth Circuit case binds the Fifth Circuit but is only persuasive elsewhere. Never anchor a multi-jurisdiction point to one circuit.
